elements.sent-2411-001: Pronounced structural changes within individual configurations (Type~I QPT), superimposed on an abrupt crossing of these configurations (Type~II QPT), define the notion of intertwined quantum phase transitions (QPTs).
elements.sent-2411-002: We discuss and present evidence for such a scenario in finite Bose and Bose-Fermi systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: The analysis is based on algebraic models with explicit configuration mixing, where the two types of QPTs describe shape-phase transitions in-between different dynamical symmetries and shape-coexistence with crossing.
elements.sent-2411-006: Iachello (Yale) on the topics considered, is acknowledged.. .
elements.sent-2411-007: Quantum phase transitions (QPTs) are qualititative changes in the properties of a physical system induced by variation of parameters in the Hamiltonian~\cite{Gilmore1978, Gilmore1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: They occur at zero temperature hence are driven by quantum fluctuations and their study is currently a topic of great interest in diverse fields~\cite{Carr2010QPT .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Such ground-state phase transitions can be categorized into two types.
elements.sent-2411-010: The first, denoted Type~I QPT, is a phase transition in a single configuration~\cite{CejJolCas10 , described by an Hamiltonian of the form~\cite{diep80 , \ba \hat H(\xi) = (1-\xi)\hat H_1 + \xi\,\hat H_2 ~, \ea where the separate Hamiltonians $\hat{H _1$ and $\hat{H _2$ are non-commuting, act in the same Hilbert space (${\cal H}$) and correspond to different phases of the system.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: When the control parameter $\xi$ changes from $0$ to $1$, the spectra and eigenstates of $\hat{H (\xi)$ progress from those of $\hat{H _1$ to those of $\hat{H _2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The number of basis states is preserved throughout the transition.
elements.sent-2411-013: A schematic illustration of the evolving spectra in a Type~I QPT is shown in Fig.~1(a).
elements.sent-2411-014: A second type of phase transition, denoted Type~II QPT, occurs when two (or more) configurations coexist~\cite{Heyde11 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In this case, the quantum Hamiltonian can be cast in matrix form~\cite{Frank2006 , \ba \hat{H (\xi_A,\xi_B,\omega) = \left [ \begin{array {cc \hat{H _{A (\xi_A) \hat{W (\omega) \hat{W (\omega) \hat{H _{B (\xi_B) \end{array \right ] ~,\quad \ea where the indices $A$ and $B$ denote the two configurations.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The terms, \bsub \ba \hat{H _A(\xi_A) = (1-\xi_A)\hat{H _{A;1 + \xi_A\,\hat{H _{A;2 ~, \hat{H _B(\xi_B) = (1-\xi_B)\hat{H _{B;1 + \xi_B\,\hat{H _{B;2 ~, \ea \esub act in different Hilbert spaces (${\cal H _A$ and ${\cal H _B$), and the mixing term $\hat{W (\omega)$ connects the two spaces.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: As the control parameters $(\xi_A,\xi_B,\omega)$ are varied, each of the sub-Hamiltonians, $\hat{H _A(\xi_A)$ and $\hat{H _B(\xi_B)$, can undergo a QPT of Type~I, and the combined Hamiltonian $\hat{H (\xi_A,\xi_B,\omega)$, which acts in the enlarged Hilbert space, ${\cal H = {\cal H _A\otimes{\cal H _B$, can experience a Type~II QPT with a crossing of the two configurations.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this case, states with distinct properties coexist at similar energies, as shown schematically in Fig.~1(b).
elements.sent-2411-019: Usually, the individual Type~I QPTs are masked by the strong mixing between the states in the separate configurations.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, if the mixing is weak, the abrupt Type~II QPT is accompanied by a distinguished Type~I QPT within each configuration separately.
elements.sent-2411-021: Such a scenario, referred to as intertwined QPTs~\cite{Gav2019,Gav2020,Gav2022 , and its manifestation in Bose and Bose-Fermi systems is the subject matter of the present contribution.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{overpic}[width=\linewidth]{fig1a-TypeI-QPT.pdf} \put (31,48) { (a) Type I QPT} \end{overpic} \begin{overpic}[width=\linewidth]{fig1b-TypeII-QPT.pdf} \put (31,50) { (b) Type II QPT} \end{overpic} \caption{\label{fig1-QPTs} Schematic illustration for the evolution of structure in a quantum phase transition (QPT). (a)~Type~I QPT, Eq.~(\ref{eq:type-i}), portraying a change from spherical-vibrator to deformed-rotor spectra within the Hilbert space of a single configuration. (b)~Type~II QPT, Eq.~(\ref{eq:type-ii}), portraying a coexistence and crossing of sets of levels associated with different configurations ($A$ and $B$). When both types of QPTs are distinguishable, they define intertwined QPTs in the extended Hilbert space.}
elements.section-2411-002: Algebraic approach to quantum phase transitions in Bose systems
elements.sent-2411-022: Algebraic models based on compact spectrum generating algebras are particularly useful for studying QPTs in mesoscopic (finite) systems.
elements.sent-2411-023: They are endowed with a rich symmetry structure, amenable for both quantum and classical analysis and provide a simple framework for tractable yet detailed calculation of observables.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a representative for this class of models in bosonic systems, we consider an interacting boson model for a single configuration and its extensions to multiple configurations.
elements.sent-2411-025: The interacting boson model (IBM)~\cite{ibm , describes low-lying quadrupole collective states in even-even nuclei in terms of $N$ interacting monopole $(s)$ and quadrupole $(d)$ bosons.}
elements.sent-2411-026: The model is based on a U(6) spectrum generating algebra with elements ${\cal G _{ij}\equiv b^{\dag _{i b_j = \{s^{\dag s,\,s^{\dag d_{m ,\, d^{\dag _{m s,\,d^{\dag _{m d_{m '}\}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The IBM Hamiltonian is expanded in terms of these generators, $\hat{H = \sum_{ij}\epsilon_{ij}\,{\cal G _{ij + \sum_{ijk\ell u_{ijk\ell}\,{\cal G _{ij {\cal G _{k\ell}$, and consists of Hermitian, rotational-invariant interactions which conserve the total number of $s$- and $d$- bosons, $\hat N = \hat{n _s + \hat{n _d = s^{\dagger s + \sum_{m d^{\dagger _{m d_{m}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: A dynamical symmetry (DS) occurs if the Hamiltonian can be written in terms of the Casimir operators of a chain of nested sub-algebras of U(6).
elements.sent-2411-029: The Hamiltonian is then completely solvable in the basis associated with each chain.
elements.sent-2411-030: The solvable limits of the IBM correspond to the following DS chains, \bsub \ba {\rm U(6)\supset U(5)\supset SO(5)\supset SO(3) \;\qquad \quad\ket{N,\, n_d,\,\tau,\,n_{\Delta ,\,L ~, {\rm U(6)\supset SU(3)\supset SO(3) \;\;\; \qquad\quad\qquad \;\;\;\ket{N,\, (\lambda,\mu),\,K,\, L ~, {\rm U(6)\supset SO(6)\supset SO(5)\supset SO(3) \qquad \;\;\,\ket{N,\, \sigma,\,\tau,\,n_{\Delta ,\, L ~.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, the quantum numbers $N,n_d,(\lambda,\mu),\sigma,\tau,L$, label the relevant irreps of U(6), U(5), SU(3), SO(6), SO(5), SO(3), respectively, and $n_{\Delta ,K$ are multiplicity labels.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The basis states are eigenstates of the DS Hamiltonian with energies expressed by the eigenvalues of the Casimir operators in a given chain.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the U(5)-DS limit, Eq.~(\ref{U5-ds ), the spectrum resembles that of a spherical vibrator, where states are arranged in U(5) $n_d$-multiplets at approximately equal energy spacing.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The spectra in the SU(3)-DS limit, Eq.~(\ref{SU3-ds ), resembles that of an axially-deformed prolate rotor with states arranged in SU(3) $(\lambda,\mu)$-multiplets forming rotational bands with $L(L+1)$-splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The lowest irrep $(2N,0)$ contains the ground band $g(K\!=\!0)$.
elements.sent-2411-037: The first excited irrep $(2N\!-\!4,2)$ contains both the $\beta(K\!=\!0)$ and $\gamma(K\!=\!2)$ bands.
elements.sent-2411-038: The lowest members of each $K$-band have $L=0,2,4\ldots$ ($L=2,3,4\ldots$) for $K\!=\!0$ ($K\!=\!2$).
elements.sent-2411-039: The spectrum in the SO(6)-DS limit of Eq.~(\ref{SO6-ds ), resembles that of a $\gamma$-unstable deformed rotor, where states are arranged in SO(6) $\sigma$-multiplets forming rotational bands with $\tau(\tau+3)$ splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The lowest irrep $\sigma\!=\!N$ contains the ground ($g$) band and the first excited irrep $\sigma\!=\!N-2$ contains the $\beta$-band.
elements.sent-2411-041: The lowest members in each band have quantum numbers $(\tau\!=\!0,\, L\!=\!0)$, $(\tau\!=\!1,\, L\!=\!2)$, $(\tau\!=\!2,\, L\!=\!2,4)$ and $(\tau\!=\!3,\, L\!=\!0,3,4,6)$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Characteristic energy ratios for the U(5), SU(3), SO(6) limits are $R_{4/2 = E(4_1)/E(2_1)=2,\,3.33,\,2.5$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Geometry is introduced in the algebraic model by means of a coset space $U(6)/U(5)\otimes U(1)$ and an energy surface, \ba E_{N (\beta,\gamma) = \langle \beta,\gamma; N\vert \hat{H \vert \beta,\gamma ; N\rangle ~, \ea obtained by the expectation value of the Hamiltonian in a `projective' coherent state~\cite{gino80,diep80 , \bsub \ba \vert\beta,\gamma ; N \rangle = (N!)^{-1/2 (b^{\dagger _{c )^N\,\vert 0\,\rangle ~, b^{\dagger _{c = (1+\beta^2)^{-1/2 [\beta\cos\gamma d^{\dagger _{0 + \beta\sin{\gamma ( d^{\dagger _{2 + d^{\dagger _{-2 )/\sqrt{2 + s^{\dagger ] ~.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-045: A microscopic interpretation of the IBM is based on its link with the nuclear shell model~\cite{IacTal87 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: According to it, the $s$ and $d$ bosons represent valence nucleon pairs with total number $N\!=\!N_{\pi +N_{\nu}$, where $N_{\pi}$ ($N_{\nu}$) is the number of proton (neutron) particle or hole pairs counted from the nearest closed shell.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The states constructed of valence nucleons are referred to as normal states.
elements.sent-2411-048: The Type~I QPTs discussed in Section~2.3.
elements.sent-2411-049: In nuclei near closed shells, additional configurations associated with particle-hole excitations across shell gaps, play a role.
elements.sent-2411-050: The resulting states are referred to as intruder states~\cite{Heyde11 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: The latter can drop in energy and coexist with normal states due to correlations induced by the residual interactions~\cite{FedPit79,HeyCas85 .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Type~II QPTs involving the crossing of normal and intruder states, can be modeled in the IBM by incorporating additional configurations with different boson numbers, in the manner described in Section~2.4.
elements.sent-2411-053: In the algebraic approach to QPTs, the dynamical symmetries correspond to possible shape-phases of the system in accord with their geometric interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-054: QPTs in a single configuration (Type~I) can be studied by an Hamiltonian $\hat{H (\xi)$, as in Eq.~(\ref{eq:type-i ), which interpolates between the different DS limits (phases) by varying the control parameter $\xi$~\cite{diep80,iaczam04 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-057: The order of the QPT is the order of the derivative where discontinuities first occur (Ehrenfest classification)~\cite{Gilmore1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: A typical Hamiltonian frequently used for Type I QPTs has the form, \begin{equation}\label{eq:ham-q} \hat H(\epsilon_d,\kappa,\chi) = \epsilon_d\, \hat n_d + \kappa\, \hat Q_\chi \cdot \hat Q_\chi~,\end{equation} where the quadrupole operator is given by, \begin{equation}\label{eq:q-op} \hat Q_\chi = d^\dag s+s^\dag \tilde d \!+\!\chi (d^\dag \times \tilde d)^{(2)} ~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: Here $\tilde d_m = (-1)^m d_{-m}$ and standard notation of angular momentum coupling is used.
elements.sent-2411-060: The control parameters $(\epsilon_d,\kappa,\chi)$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ham-q with values $(\kappa\!=\!0)$, $(\epsilon_d\!=\!0,\chi\!=\!-\sqrt{7 /2)$ and $(\epsilon_d\!=\!0,\chi\!=\!0)$, interpolate between the respective U(5), SU(3) and SO(6) DS~limits.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: For the Hamiltonian~\eqref{eq:ham-q , the associated Landau potential~(\ref{enesurf ) reads, \ba E_{N (\beta,\gamma;\epsilon_d,\kappa,\chi) = 5\kappa\, N + \frac{N\beta^2 {1+\beta^2 \left[\epsilon_d + \kappa (\chi^2-4)\right] + \frac{N(N-1)\beta^2 {(1+\beta^2)^2}\kappa \left[4 - 4\bar{\chi}\beta\,\cos3\gamma + \bar\chi^2\beta^2\right] ~, \ea where $\bar\chi\!=\!\sqrt{\frac{2 {7 }\chi$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The U(5)-SU(3) transition is found to be first-order, the U(5)-SO(6) transition is second order and the SU(3)-SO(6) transition is a crossover.

elements.sent-2411-064: The latter converges in the large-$N$ limit to the classical order parameter related to the equilibrium deformation $\beta_{\rm eq}$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:order-param} \frac{\braket{\hat n_d}_{0^+_1}}{N} \approx \frac{\beta_{\rm eq}^2}{1+\beta_{\rm eq}^2}~.\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-005: IBM-CM in the Zirconium chain of isotopes
elements.sent-2411-082: The IBM-CM framework has been used in~\cite{Gav2019,Gav2020,Gav2022 to calculate spectral properties of low-lying states in $^{92-110}$Zr.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In a shell model approach, $_{40 ^{90}$Zr is taken as a core and valence neutrons in the 50--82 major shell.}

elements.section-2411-003: Algebraic approach to quantum phase transitions in Bose-Fermi systems
elements.sent-2411-134: The study of QPTs in a Bose-Fermi system is more complex due to the presence of both collective (bosonic) and single-particle (fermionic) degrees of freedom with different statistics.
elements.sent-2411-135: As a representative for the algebraic approach to such systems, we consider the interacting boson-fermion model (IBFM)~\cite{IBFMBook , a model of odd-even nuclei phrased in terms of correlated pairs with $L=0,2$ ($s,d$ bosons) and unpaired particles with angular momentum~$j$ ($j$~fermions).}
elements.sent-2411-136: The model is based on a $U_{b (6)\otimes U_{f (\Omega)$ spectrum generating algebra, where $U_{b (6)$ is the bosonic algebra of the IBM discussed in Section~2.1 and $U_{f (\Omega)$ is the fermion algebra with $\Omega$ the dimension of the single-fermion space.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Here we focus the discussion to the case of a single-$j$ fermion for which $\Omega=2j+1$.
elements.sent-2411-138: The IBFM Hamiltonian contains boson- fermion- and boson-fermion parts, \ba \hat H = \hat H_{\rm b + \hat H_{\rm f + \hat V_{\rm bf ~, \ea where, \bsub \ba \hat H_{\rm b (\epsilon_d,\kappa,\chi) = \epsilon_d\, \hat n_d + \kappa\, \hat Q_\chi \cdot \hat Q_\chi ~, \hat H_{\rm f (\epsilon_j) = \epsilon_j\,\hat{n _j ~, \hat V_{\rm bf (A,\Gamma,\Lambda) = V_{\rm bf ^{\rm MON + \hat{V _{\rm bf ^{\rm QUAD + \hat{V _{\rm bf ^{\rm EXC ~.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-206: We have presented the notion of intertwined quantum phase transitions (QPTs) and considered its manifestation in a system of bosons and in a mixed system of bosons and fermions.
elements.sent-2411-207: Intertwined QPTs correspond to a situation of a QPT involving the crossing of two (or more) configurations (Type~II), each of which is in itself undergoing a QPT (Type~I).
elements.sent-2411-208: As a case study of intertwined QPTs in a finite Bose system, we have employed the algebraic interacting boson model with configuration mixing, describing collective states in even-even nuclei in terms of bosons.
elements.sent-2411-209: A comprehensive analysis of spectral properties of Zr isotopes, including the evolution of structure, order parameters and related observables, revealed the role of two configurations.
elements.sent-2411-210: The normal configuration remained spherical along the Zr chain, while the intruder configuration experienced first a transition from spherical [U(5)] to an axially-deformed [SU(3)] shape, and then a crossover to a $\gamma$-unstable [SO(6)] shape.
elements.sent-2411-211: These gradual shape-phase transitions involve a change of symmetries and are superimposed on an abrupt crossing of the two configurations.
elements.sent-2411-212: The normal-intruder mixing is weak (apart from the crossing point), ensuring that both types of QPTs are distinguishable, thus demonstrating intertwined QPTs in even-even nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-213: As a case study of intertwined QPTs in a finite Bose-Fermi system, we have employed the algebraic interacting boson-fermion model with configuration mixing, which describes low-lying states in odd-even nuclei in terms of ground and core-excited bosonic configurations coupled to a single-fermion.
elements.sent-2411-214: An application to the Nb isotopes disclosed a Type~II QPT (abrupt crossing of normal and intruder states) accompanied by a Type~I QPT (gradual shape evolution and transition from weak to strong coupling within the intruder configuration), thus demonstrating intertwined QPTs in odd-even nuclei.
elements.sent-2411-215: The observed intertwined QPTs in the odd-mass Nb isotopes echo the intertwined QPTs of the adjacent even-even Zr isotopes.
elements.sent-2411-216: The results reported in the present contribution motivate further experiments on the spectroscopy of non-yrast states in even-even and odd-even nuclei, as well as set the path for new investigations on multiple and intertwined QPTs in other Bose and Bose-Fermi systems.
